reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide generation; loss of H2O from ethyl nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: All reagents are compatible at room temperature and must be premixed in DES before entering the heated zone; nitrile oxide forms only at elevated temperature, so no premature reaction occurs. Single feed simplifies pumping of viscous DES.

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ at 120°C; 2 equiv used to drive conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system; acts as reaction medium and may promote nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide intermediate) in situ at 120°C
Regioselective [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide 1,3-dipole with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate

key_intermediate: ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate)

stream_logic.reasoning: All components are stable together at room temperature. Nitrile oxide generation requires 120°C, so premixing is safe. Single stream avoids challenges of pumping viscous DES through multiple lines and ensures correct stoichiometry. Concentration: phenylacetylene 0.5 M, ethyl nitroacetate 1.0 M in 1 mL DES.

mixing_order_reasoning: Both substrates are dissolved in the DES at room temperature before entering the heated coil reactor. No premature reaction occurs because nitrile oxide generation requires elevated temperature. A single premixed feed is optimal to avoid the complexity of mixing two viscous DES streams and ensures homogeneous composition entering the heated zone.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; the reaction proceeds via a concerted pericyclic mechanism under air.
quench_reasoning: The batch protocol quenches with water only for workup/extraction purposes. In flow, the product stream can be collected directly and worked up offline. No reactive intermediates persist after the heated zone at full conversion.
wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition. The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (thermal elimination of H2O vs. DES-mediated pathway) is not fully characterized in literature — assumed thermal. Pumping viscous TBAB/EG DES may require heated feed lines or dilution; viscosity at RT could be problematic for standard HPLC pumps. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests nitrile oxide dimerization is competitive, which flow's rapid mixing and heating could mitigate. Superheating strategy assumes PFA tubing with appropriate back-pressure regulator.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides three key advantages: (1) Superior heat transfer in narrow-bore tubing (e.g., 1 mm ID PFA coil) enables rapid heating to 120°C, eliminating the thermal ramp time of an oil bath and reducing effective residence time below 15 min. (2) Back-pressure regulation (e.g., 10–17 bar) allows superheating above the boiling point of any volatile components, potentially enabling higher temperatures (140–150°C) for further rate acceleration. (3) Continuous generation and immediate trapping of the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate minimizes dimerization to furoxan, potentially improving yield beyond 83%. The viscous DES medium benefits from the high surface-to-volume ratio for efficient heat transfer. A 1 mm ID × 3 m PFA coil (~2.4 mL volume) at 0.5 mL/min flow rate gives ~5 min residence time, targeting 2–3× τ reduction.
